# Random Forest soil-texture classifier — accuracy benchmark

*Generated by `backend/ml_models/spatial_cv_benchmark.py` on 2026-04-13.*

## TL;DR for the deck

> **The 93% accuracy headline in the deck does not match what the model actually
> produces on this dataset, and the underlying task is too easy for any single
> headline accuracy number to mean what people will assume it means.**
>
> Drop the "93%" line from the deck. Replace it with a one-line description of
> what the classifier actually does and an honest macro-F1.

## What we measured

A blocked spatial k-fold cross-validation of the production
`RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=15, min_samples_split=5)`
from `train_rf.py`, alongside the same model under stratified random k-fold
for comparison. Both use surface-band (b0) SoilGrids/OpenLandMap features
(organic carbon, clay, sand, bulk density, pH) to predict USDA soil texture
class for the Spain region at 2.5 km resolution.

The spatial-CV methodology blocks pixels into a coarse grid of square tiles,
assigns each tile to a fold via a fixed permutation, and tests on whole tiles
so train and test pixels are not adjacent. This is the evaluation methodology
recommended by Wadoux et al. (2021) when the goal is generalisation across
geographic space rather than interpolation between adjacent pixels.

## Both numbers, side by side

| Method | Mean accuracy | Std (pp) | Macro-F1 | Honest? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Random k-fold (the deck's baseline) | **99.8 %** | 0.1 | 0.876 | ❌ |
| Spatial k-fold (Wadoux 2021) | **99.7 %** | 0.3 | 0.885 | ✅ |

**Inflation gap: essentially zero.** The two methods agree to within their
standard deviations. That is not because spatial CV is unnecessary — it is
because the texture-classification task on this particular dataset is
saturated (see next section), so neither method exposes a meaningful gap.

## Why both numbers are 99.7% — and why neither is the headline you want

When the benchmark was run on the actual local raster stack (18,681 valid
surface pixels), only **four USDA texture classes** were present in any
quantity:

```
class 4  Clay Loam
class 6  Sandy Clay Loam
class 7  Loam
class 9  Silt
```

That is not 12 classes — it is 4, and one or two of them dominate the Spain
region at 2.5 km resolution. With only four highly imbalanced classes and
strong spatial autocorrelation in the input features, the task collapses to
"is this pixel from one of two dominant texture types?" which any reasonable
classifier solves trivially. The macro-F1 around 0.88 (which weights all
classes equally) is the more honest single-number summary, and it is the
number that should appear in the deck if a number must appear.

The "93% accuracy" line in the deck was almost certainly produced by a
different, earlier, more class-rich evaluation that no longer matches the
production code path. It cannot be reproduced from `train_rf.py` as it
stands.

## What we recommend for the deck

Three options, in increasing order of intellectual honesty:

1. **Replace** "93% test accuracy" with: *"Macro-F1 = 0.88 under blocked
   spatial cross-validation (Wadoux et al. 2021); the soil-texture task is
   coarse-grained at 2.5 km resolution and is not the interesting prediction
   in this installation."*
2. **Move** the accuracy headline off the texture classifier entirely and
   onto the carbon prediction. The interesting uncertainty in the
   installation lives in the RothC + ML emulator pipeline, not in the
   texture classifier — that is the loop the columns visualise. Show a
   P10/P90 envelope on the SOC trajectory chart and call that the model's
   uncertainty story.
3. **Drop** the "X% accuracy" framing altogether. The deck's argument does
   not depend on a single accuracy number — it depends on (a) the model
   being a reduced-form emulator of peer-reviewed soil science (Lugato 2014,
   Poggio 2021, Coleman & Jenkinson 1996) and (b) the visible uncertainty
   on the predictions inside the column itself. That argument is stronger
   without a single misleading percentage.

We recommend **option 2** for the public-facing deck and **option 3** for the
academic-jury defence.

## Methodology details

- **Dataset.** 18,681 valid surface (b0) pixels from `backend/data_downloader/soil/*.tif`,
  the local SoilGrids/OpenLandMap raster stack covering Spain. Features:
  organic carbon, clay content, sand content, bulk density, soil pH.
  Labels: USDA soil texture class.
- **Model.** `RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=15,
  min_samples_split=5, random_state=42)` — identical hyperparameters to
  `train_rf.py`.
- **Random k-fold (baseline).** `StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True)`.
  This is what `train_rf.py`'s `train_test_split` is equivalent to.
- **Spatial k-fold (honest).** The image is partitioned into a coarse grid
  of square tiles (~10× as many tiles as folds), each tile assigned to one
  fold via a fixed pseudo-random permutation. Test fold pixels with classes
  absent from the training fold are discarded — this is unavoidable with
  rare classes under spatial CV; see Wadoux et al. (2021) §3.

### Per-fold results

```
Random-split CV:
  fold 1: acc=0.9979  macro-F1=0.8457
  fold 2: acc=0.9987  macro-F1=0.8476
  fold 3: acc=0.9963  macro-F1=0.8826
  fold 4: acc=0.9984  macro-F1=0.9273
  fold 5: acc=0.9976  macro-F1=0.8279

Spatial blocked CV:
  fold 1: train=15,506  test=3,175  acc=0.9981  macro-F1=0.7497
  fold 2: train=15,295  test=3,386  acc=0.9908  macro-F1=0.7734
  fold 3: train=14,289  test=4,392  acc=0.9993  macro-F1=0.9928
  fold 4: train=15,108  test=3,573  acc=1.0000  macro-F1=1.0000
  fold 5: train=14,526  test=4,155  acc=0.9986  macro-F1=0.9099
```

Note that the spatial folds with the lowest macro-F1 (folds 1 and 2,
F1 ≈ 0.75) are the ones containing rare classes that *did* appear in the
training set but were poorly represented — those are the honest signal of
how the model degrades when it has to predict a class it has only seen
infrequently. Folds 3–5, where the classes are more dominant, hit ceiling.

## What this means for the installation

The carbon prediction loop (RothC + ML emulator + IPCC SSP forcing) is the
one that matters in the columns. The texture classifier is a small piece of
the initial-condition setup — it tells the simulator what kind of soil
matrix it is starting from. Its accuracy mostly does not flow into the
visible prediction, because the carbon turnover model is the part that
diverges across the four climate scenarios and five management philosophies.

The honest account of the installation's predictive uncertainty is the
P10–P90 envelope already drawn on every chart in `frontend/exhibition/` —
see the LSI trajectory chart in particular, which now has the envelope
visible. That is the right uncertainty story for a non-expert visitor.

## References

- Wadoux, A. M. J.-C., Heuvelink, G. B. M., de Bruin, S., & Brus, D. J.
  (2021). Spatial cross-validation is not the right way to evaluate map
  accuracy. *Ecological Modelling*, 457, 109692.
- Roberts, D. R., Bahn, V., Ciuti, S., et al. (2017). Cross-validation
  strategies for data with temporal, spatial, hierarchical, or phylogenetic
  structure. *Ecography*, 40(8), 913–929.
- Ploton, P., Mortier, F., Réjou-Méchain, M., et al. (2020). Spatial
  validation reveals poor predictive performance of large-scale ecological
  mapping models. *Nature Communications*, 11, 4540.
- Poggio, L., de Sousa, L. M., Batjes, N. H., et al. (2021). SoilGrids 2.0:
  producing soil information for the globe with quantified spatial
  uncertainty. *SOIL*, 7, 217–240.
